title: Chapter 23: Tea with Mrs. Hendricks
content: The knock comes at 4:47 PM, which is weird, because nobody knocks at 4:47 PM. Deliveries come at noon. Maya's friends just text "here" from the lobby. Dave doesn't knock — he just opens the door and announces himself like a herald.
Chloe lifts her head from the heating vent. The knock comes again. Firmer this time. Deliberate.
Maya opens the door.

Mrs. Hendricks is standing in the hallway. She is not wearing her trench coat. This is so fundamentally wrong that Chloe does a double-take, which for a capybara involves blinking both eyes a full second apart and tilting her head three degrees to the left.

The trench coat is gone. In its place: a cream cardigan with small pearl buttons. Her hair, usually pinned into a helmet of authority, is looser. Softer. She looks like a different person. She looks like a person, actually — that's the thing. The trench coat was a uniform. Without it, Mrs. Hendricks is just a woman in a cardigan, and she is holding a paper bag of oranges and a tin of shortbread cookies, and she is looking at Maya with an expression Chloe has never seen on her face before.
Uncertainty.
"I thought your animal might like these," Mrs. Hendricks says. She holds up the bag of oranges like it's a peace offering. Which, Chloe realizes, it probably is.
Maya blinks. "Mrs. Hendricks, are you — did you want to come in?"
A pause. A long one. The kind of pause that has weather in it.
"Yes," Mrs. Hendricks says. "I think I would."

Maya makes tea because Maya makes tea for everything. It's her love language, her coping mechanism, and her excuse to use the electric kettle that was definitely not designed for a studio apartment. She sets out three mugs — three, which is notable, because Mrs. Hendricks has never been offered a beverage in this apartment before. And then Maya retreats to the kitchenette to give them space, because Maya is smart like that.
Mrs. Hendricks sits on the edge of the couch like it might eject her. Her back is ramrod straight. Her ankles are crossed. Her hands rest on her knees. She looks like she's waiting for a deposition.
Chloe considers the situation.

Mrs. Hendricks has been the antagonist of this apartment for months. The violations. The inspections. The clipboard. That time she stood in the doorway and counted Chloe's pellets like they were contraband. She has been, objectively, a lot.

But she's also been alone. Chloe has seen it in ways that humans miss — the particular stillness of an apartment where only one person lives, the soundprint of a space that never has laughter in it. Chloe can hear through the walls. She knows what silence sounds like when it has weight.

Chloe walks over. Slowly. Making no sudden movements, because Mrs. Hendricks is skittish, and Chloe knows about skittish. She has mastered the art of approaching anxious creatures, mostly because she is one.
She lies down next to Mrs. Hendricks' feet.
Mrs. Hendricks goes very still. Not the still of hostility. The still of someone who doesn't want to break something fragile.
Then, slowly — so slowly — her hand reaches down.
Her fingers touch the fur on Chloe's back. Just the tips at first. Tentative. Testing.
Chloe hums.

Not the big hum. Not the full-body resonance that knocks negative energy out of orbit. Just a small one. A greeting hum. A "you're welcome here" hum. The hum that says I see you and I am soft and you can touch me and nothing bad will happen.
Mrs. Hendricks' eyes close.
She talks.

Chloe didn't expect her to talk. Mrs. Hendricks has never seemed like a talker. She's a declarer. A proclaimer. A "this is a violation" and "you have until Thursday" kind of communicator. But something about the hum — or the cardigan, or the oranges, or the fact that someone just let her in without asking what she wanted — opens a door.

"My husband died eight years ago," she says. "Robert. He was a teacher. Ninth grade biology. You would have liked him — he had a calm about him." She pauses. "I did not have a calm. That was always the joke. He was the calm, I was the organization. Together we worked. Without him…"
She doesn't finish the sentence. Her hand keeps moving through Chloe's fur. Steady. Rhythmic. Like she's counting but not counting violations — counting heartbeats.

"I've owned this building since 1979," she says. "Robert and I bought it together. The neighborhood was different then. Dangerous. People told us we were crazy. But we weren't crazy — we just believed that things get better if you stay. So we stayed."
Her voice shifts. Gets rougher around the edges, like fabric worn thin at the elbows.

"Now it's expensive. That's a different kind of dangerous, you know. The dangerous before was — you could get hurt walking home. The dangerous now is — you can't afford to walk home. The bodega on the corner closed last month. Became a — what is it — a matcha place. Twelve dollars for green milk."
From the kitchenette, Maya makes a sound that is part laugh, part grief.

"Robert would have hated the matcha," Mrs. Hendricks says. And then she does something Chloe has never seen before. She smiles. It's small, and it's sad, and it transforms her entire face into something young.

She keeps talking. About the building — the tenants she's had, the ones who stayed for decades, the ones who left in the night without paying. About the garden she tried to start on the roof, how the wind ate everything but the rosemary. About the stray cat who lived in the alley for six years, how she fed it every morning, how one day it just didn't come back.

She doesn't say the word lonely. But Chloe hears it. She hears it in the inventory of days — wake at 5:30, coffee, walk the building halls, check the mailroom, clean the laundry machines even though the laundry machines have a service, go back upstairs, eat lunch alone, watch the news, watch the news again because the quiet is too loud, check the hallway again, dinner alone, bed at 9:00 because there is no reason to stay up.

Nobody calls. Nobody visits. The only time anyone speaks to her is when something is wrong — a leak, a noise, a violation. She has become a function. A building manager. A sign on a door. She has become the thing she does, not the person she is.
Chloe rests her head on Mrs. Hendricks' shoe. A small brown head on a sensible black flat. The most gentle pressure. I'm here. I'm here. I'm here.
Mrs. Hendricks strokes her fur. The hum fills the room like sunlight through a window — not demanding, not loud, just present. Just warm.

Maya comes back with the tea. She sets it down without speaking. Sits in the chair across from them. Folds her legs underneath her. And for a long time, nobody says anything, which is the best thing anyone could say.

Mrs. Hendricks leaves at 6:23 PM. She has been in the apartment for almost two hours. This is longer than all her previous visits combined, including the Great Inspection of February and the Pellet Counting Incident.

She stands at the door. She looks different than when she came in. Not younger — that's not how it works. But less armored. The cardigan isn't a costume; it's just a cardigan. The woman inside it is visible.
"I'll bring more oranges tomorrow," she says.
And then — almost, nearly, not quite — she smiles.
The door closes.
Maya looks at Chloe. Chloe looks at Maya.
"Did that just happen?" Maya asks.
Chloe hums.
Maya sits down on the floor next to her. "She brought oranges. And shortbread. From a *tin*." She shakes her head. "I thought she was going to evict us, like, every single time she knocked."

Chloe blinks slowly. She understands something that humans have trouble with, which is that meanness is almost always loneliness wearing a loud jacket. That the people who inspect and violation and clipboard you are usually the ones who have nobody to go home to. That the hardest shells are covering the softest insides, and sometimes it just takes a capybara and a hum and a bag of oranges to crack them open.
Maya eats a shortbread cookie. She offers one to Chloe, who takes it with careful lips because shortbread is precious and must be savored.
The apartment is quiet. The good kind of quiet. The kind that happens after someone tells you the truth and you didn't run away.

Outside, the city does its city things — horns and sirens and the matcha place blending twelve-dollar green milk. But in here, there is a capybara with cookie crumbs on her chin and a human who is looking at her phone to text Elif: "Mrs. Hendricks just had tea with us. I know. I KNOW."

Chloe settles back onto the heating vent. The warmth is good. The warm is always good. But there's a different kind of warm now, and it lives in the space where a mean old landlady turned out to be a lonely old woman who just needed someone to sit next to.
Tomorrow there will be more oranges.
Tomorrow there will be more Mrs. Hendricks.
And Chloe will be here. Humming. Head on a shoe. No thoughts, just fluff, and the quiet understanding that the hardest people to love are usually the ones who need it most.
